era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt is in terminal decline; the Sixth Dynasty (c. 2278–2184 BCE) is fragmenting into regional power bases. Simultaneously, the Akkad Empire under Naram-Sin (r. 2254–2218 BCE) dominates Mesopotamia and the Levant. In the Indus Valley Civilization, mature urban centres (Harappa, Mohenjo-daro) are at their height. The Early Bronze Age III is well established across the Eastern Mediterranean and Anatolia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
Northern Europe (pre-literate societies, sparse archaeological coverage)
East Asia beyond the Shang dynasty region (limited contemporary records)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin at zenith of territorial expansion
events.description: Naram-Sin (r. 2254–2218 BCE) consolidates Akkadian control over Mesopotamia and extends campaigns into the Levant, Anatolia, and possibly the Zagros Mountains. He declares himself a god and establishes a new model of divine kingship. This period represents the apex of Akkadian territorial reach before the empire's rapid collapse around 2218 BCE, likely triggered by climate stress and internal rebellion.

sources.name: Naram-Sin Stele (Victory Stele of Naram-Sin), limestone relief from Susa
Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative texts from Nippur and Lagash
Historiographic consensus from Akkadian studies (Liverani, Postgate)

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested in contemporary inscriptions and the Naram-Sin Stele. His regnal dates are secure within ±5 years via synchronism with Egyptian records and astronomical data.

events.title: Egyptian Sixth Dynasty in advanced fragmentation; regional governors consolidate power
events.description: By 2129 BCE, the Sixth Dynasty (c. 2278–2184 BCE) is severely weakened. Pepi II (r. c. 2278–2184 BCE, though some scholars place him earlier) has reigned for an exceptionally long period, and central authority has devolved to regional nomarchs (governors). The Old Kingdom's characteristic pyramid-building and centralized administration have collapsed. This period marks the transition to the First Intermediate Period (c. 2181–2055 BCE).

sources.name: Abydos King List and Turin Papyrus (later compilations with traditional dating)
Autobiographical inscriptions of nomarchs (e.g., Ankhtifi, Sirenput) from Upper Egypt
Archaeological evidence of reduced pyramid construction and administrative decline

events.certainty_note: Pepi II's reign length and exact dates are disputed. Some Egyptologists place him c. 2278–2184 BCE; others compress his reign. The fragmentation of the Sixth Dynasty is archaeologically clear, but precise dating within this period is uncertain (±10–15 years).
cross_references: Part of broader Old Kingdom decline (c. 2150–2050 BCE)

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization at mature urban peak; extensive trade networks active
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization (c. 2600–1900 BCE) is in its mature phase. Major urban centres such as Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira, and Kalibangan are fully developed with standardized weights, measures, and seals. Long-distance trade with Mesopotamia, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia is well-established. The civilization is characterized by planned urban layouts, advanced drainage systems, and undeciphered script on seals.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, and other Indus sites
Indus seals and standardized weights (material evidence)
Mesopotamian texts mentioning Meluhha (likely Indus Valley)

events.certainty_note: The mature Indus Valley Civilization is archaeologically well-attested. Absolute dating relies on stratigraphic correlation and radiocarbon; the mature phase is dated c. 2600–1900 BCE with reasonable confidence (±50 years).

disconfirming_evidence: Pepi II's reign length is highly disputed. Traditional king lists assign him 64 years; modern scholars debate whether this is accurate or inflated. Some Egyptologists place him much earlier (c. 2310–2260 BCE) or compress his reign to 30–40 years. The exact dating of the Sixth Dynasty's collapse and the transition to the First Intermediate Period remains uncertain (±10–20 years). Naram-Sin's deification and the extent of his territorial control are also debated; some scholars argue his inscriptions exaggerate his reach.
historiographic_note: This period is documented through royal inscriptions, administrative texts, and archaeology, but absolute chronology is uncertain. Egyptian dating relies on king lists compiled centuries later and astronomical calculations with inherent margins of error. Mesopotamian chronology is anchored to Akkadian inscriptions but depends on synchronisms with Egypt. The Indus Valley Civilization has no written historical narrative; understanding comes entirely from archaeology and material culture. All three regions' chronologies are subject to ±10–20 year shifts depending on methodological choices (radiocarbon calibration, Nile flood records, etc.). Western-centric scholarship has historically privileged Egypt and Mesopotamia; the Indus Valley's sophistication was underappreciated until the 20th century.

graph_edges.note: Naram-Sin's overextension and climate stress contributed to Akkad's rapid collapse within a generation.
Sixth Dynasty fragmentation directly led to the First Intermediate Period and eventual Middle Kingdom reunification.
Akkadian and Indus Valley civilizations were contemporaries; Akkadian texts mention trade with Meluhha (Indus Valley).
